summary: Charity Majors argues that while the tech industry has rightly created alternatives to management as the only career path, engineering managers remain critically important as force multipliers. She makes the unconventional case that the best reason to try management isn't career advancement but personal growth — management forces you to develop foundational life skills like self-regulation, self-awareness, hard conversations, and boundary-setting that improve all your relationships.
key_points: The industry has evolved: staff+ engineering tracks now exist, so people no longer need to become managers for career progression or compensation
Good engineering managers are force multipliers — the biggest obstacles to productivity are organizational, not technical
Management forces you to develop deep interpersonal skills (self-regulation, self-awareness, boundary-setting, hard conversations) that transfer to all of life
Work is actually an ideal sandbox for practicing life skills because the social contract is more explicit and the stakes are lower than personal relationships
Technical skills decay but people skills are persistent and increase your optionality for life
money_quotes: Work is always about two things: what you put out into the world, and who you become while doing it.
A team with a good engineering manager will build circles around a team without one. The larger or more complex the org or the product, and the faster you want to move, the more true this is.

Work is actually kind of an ideal sandbox for life skills, because the social contract is more explicit. These are structured relationships, with rules and conventions and expectations, and your purpose for coming together is clear.
Managers don't make all the decisions, but they do ensure the decisions get made.
Technical skills do decay and become obsolete, particularly language fluency, but people skills do not. Once you have built these muscles, you will carry them with you for life.
key_insight: The most compelling reason to try engineering management isn't career advancement but that it forces you to develop foundational interpersonal skills — self-regulation, self-awareness, hard conversations, boundary-setting — that make you better at all of life's relationships.
